text: Every national legal system must establish rigorous frameworks to manage threats that jeopardize the state’s stability or very existence. These extraordinary circumstances, defined as states of emergency, shift primary responsibility for public safety to state administration bodies. To ensure effective governance during crises, legislative provisions must clearly delineate the scope of authority and liability for these entities, ensuring that emergency powers are invoked only when standard constitutional measures prove inadequate. This article examines the evolution of emergency legal institutions in the newly independent Polish state, focusing on the frameworks established by the March Constitution of 1921 and the April Constitution of 1935. Additionally, it analyzes subsidiary legal acts from this era that were pivotal in safeguarding national security. By employing dogmatic-legal and historical-legal methodologies, this study explores how specific regulations were justified by the shifting internal and external pressures of the interwar period, illustrating the legal transition from precarious independence to centralized crisis management.
